narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.50—above the typical 3.21 average—and an LSAT score of 35.50, you brought some strong academic credentials to your law school application, along with your upper-middle family income background and plans to attend full-time like most students. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your probability of failure at 57.6%, tipping the scales just enough to result in a rejection. This prediction hinges on how your profile stacks up against patterns from thousands of past students, focusing on key indicators of bar success. The most influential factor pushing toward a failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, well below the average of 5.53, signaling to the model that your later undergraduate performance placed you in the lower tier compared to peers who typically pass the bar. Counterbalancing this somewhat was your LSAT score, which pulled the prediction in a positive direction toward passing, as it's close to the solid average of 36.70 and reflects good analytical skills. Your undergraduate GPA also helped your case, nudging the model toward expecting a pass since it's stronger than the typical 3.21, showing consistent prior achievement. Overall, while your strengths in LSAT and GPA mitigated some risk, the standout lower Year 3 decile carried the most weight in convincing the model you'd face challenges on the bar. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from an upper-middle background—many in similar situations succeed with targeted effort—but the model prioritizes these academic deciles as the strongest bar exam predictors. To move forward, consider retaking courses or building a stronger late-undergrad record to boost that Year 3 decile, perhaps through post-bac programs or additional certifications that highlight improvement. You might also seek feedback from admissions counselors on bolstering weaker areas or applying to programs with holistic reviews beyond predictive models. With your above-average GPA and near-average LSAT as a foundation, focusing on that key decile gap could dramatically shift future outcomes. Keep pushing; many students turn rejections into bar-passing success stories by addressing exactly these patterns.
